title: Localisation and compactness properties of the Navier-Stokes global regularity problem

def:1.1.summary: Smooth solution categories for the NSE: defines smooth data $(u_0$, f, T), finite energy (E $< \infty), H^1$ norm, Schwartz class, and periodic data with period L. The hierarchy Schwartz $\subset H^1 \subset$ finite energy, with periodic incompatible with all three unless trivial. Fixes notation for all subsequent conjectures.

conjecture:1.9.summary: Global well-posedness in periodic $H^1$: for any periodic $H^1$ data, there exists a smooth periodic $H^1$ solution on all of [0,T]. The paper's primary sufficient hypothesis — the strongest assumption from which all major implication chains flow.

conjecture:1.10.summary: A priori periodic $H^1$ bound: any periodic $H^1$ mild solution on [0,T] satisfies ‖u‖$_{X^1} \leF(H^1(u_0,f,T)$, T) for some finite function F. Equivalent to conjecture:1.9 by thm:1.20.

conjecture:1.13.summary: Global almost regularity for homogeneous $H^1$ data on $R^3$: any smooth homogeneous $H^1$ initial data produces a global almost smooth $H^1$ solution. Implied by conjecture:1.19; equivalent to conjecture:1.14, 1.15, 1.16, 1.17 by thm:1.20.

conjecture:1.15.summary: Global well-posedness in homogeneous $H^1$: for any homogeneous $H^1$ data, there exists a global mild $H^1$ solution. Equivalent to conjecture:1.13 and conjecture:1.16 by thm:1.20 via concentration compactness.

conjecture:1.19.summary: Global well-posedness from spatially smooth $H^1$ data: any $H^1$ initial data that is also spatially smooth (all spatial derivatives bounded) produces a global smooth solution. Implied by conjecture:1.9 via thm:12.2; implies conjecture:1.13 and conjecture:1.5.

conjecture:1.5.summary: Global regularity for Schwartz data (Clay Millennium Prize positive formulation): every Schwartz initial datum with Schwartz forcing produces a global smooth solution. Implied by conjecture:1.19, hence ultimately by conjecture:1.9.

thm:1.20.summary: MAIN: Establishes the implication diagram between NSE conjecture variants. Key chains: (1) conj:1.9 ↔ conj:1.10; (2) conj:$1.9 \to$ conj:$1.8 \to$ conj:1.4; (3) conj:$1.9 \to$ conj:$1.19 \to$ conj:1.13 and conj:1.5; (4) conj:1.13 ↔ conj:1.14 ↔ conj:1.15 ↔ conj:1.16 ↔ conj:1.17. Upshot: periodic $H^1$ well-posedness implies both Clay Prize formulations.

thm:1.12.summary: Non-existence: there exists smooth $H^1$ initial data $u_0$ for which no smooth finite energy solution exists for any T > 0. Shows the mild $H^1$ framework is necessary — smooth $H^1$ data alone does not guarantee smooth solutions.

prop:1.7.summary: Equivalence of forced and unforced periodic regularity: conjecture:1.6 (global regularity for periodic data with forcing) is equivalent to conjecture:1.4 (homogeneous periodic). The forcing term can be absorbed by homogenisation (lem:6.1).

lem:2.1.summary: Linear and bilinear estimates for the heat semigroup in $X^s$ spaces. Standard energy estimate (eq:21): ‖u‖$_{X^s}$ ≲ ‖$u(t_0)$‖$_{H^s} +$ ‖F‖$_{L^1_t H^s_x}$; $L^2$ variant (eq:22); bilinear product estimate ‖$\nabla(uv)$‖$_{L^4_t L^2_x}$ ≲ ‖u‖$_{X^1}$‖v‖$_{X^1} (eq$:24). Fundamental tool used in nearly every subsequent argument.

lem:2.2.summary: Heat kernel decay: for f Fourier-supported on annulus ${|\xi|$ ~ N}, ‖$e^{t\Delta}f$‖$_{L^q}$ ≲ $t^{3/2q−3/2p}$ exp(−$ctN^2)$ ‖f‖$_{L^p}$. Exponential frequency-scale decay used in high-frequency energy and enstrophy arguments.

lem:4.1.summary: Reduction to normalised pressure: every finite energy almost smooth solution can be written with pressure p = −$\Delta^{-1}\partial_i\partial_j(u_iu_j) + \Delta^{-1}\nabla\cdotf +$ C(t) (plus an $x\cdota(t)$ term in the periodic case). Canonical pressure form required for the enstrophy equation derivation in sec:10.

cor:4.3.summary: Almost smooth $H^1$ solutions are essentially mild: any almost smooth $H^1$ solution with normalised pressure is automatically a mild $H^1$ solution. Reduces almost-smooth to mild $H^1$ for uniqueness and enstrophy localisation arguments.

thm:5.1.summary: Local well-posedness in periodic $H^1$: (i) strong solution (u $\in C^0_t H^1_x)$; (ii) local existence for small (‖$u_0$‖$_{H^1}^4$ T $\le$ c); (iii) uniqueness; (iv) smooth data yields smooth solution; (v) Lipschitz stability in $X^1$. Foundation for all conditional proofs involving periodic data.

cor:5.2.summary: Maximal Cauchy development for periodic $H^1$: either global smooth solution exists, or there is a blowup time T_* where ‖u(t)‖$_{H^1} \to \infty$. Characterises the blowup scenario needed for the proof by contradiction in the implication proofs.

prop:5.3.summary: Compactness of periodic $H^1$ solutions: weakly convergent data sequences produce weakly convergent solutions in $X^1$, with strong convergence on $[\tau,T]$. Essential for the homogenisation limit (sec:6) and for passing to limits in implication proofs.

thm:5.4.summary: Local well-posedness in $H^1 (non-periodic, R^3)$: same five properties as thm:5.1 — strong solution, local existence, uniqueness, regularity (Schwartz data yields smooth solution), Lipschitz stability. The non-periodic LWP base used throughout sec:11–15.

prop:5.6.summary: Almost regularity: homogeneous $H^1$ mild solutions satisfying the small-data condition (‖$u_0$‖$_{H^1}^4$ T $\le$ c) are smooth on $(\tau,T] \times R^3$; if $u_0$ is smooth, the solution is almost smooth. Used in prop:15.1 to construct non-smooth-from-smooth data for thm:1.12.

lem:6.1.summary: Irrational shift homogenisation: if $\alpha \in R^3/Z^3$ is irrational $(k\cdot\alpha \ne 0$ mod 1 for all k $\ne 0)$, then the spatially shifted forcing $f_{\lambda\alpha}(x)$ :$= f(x+\lambda\alpha)$ converges weakly to 0 in $L^\infty_t H^1_x$ as $\lambda \to \infty$. Allows conversion of periodic inhomogeneous problems to homogeneous ones for prop:1.7.

lem:8.1.summary: Global energy inequality: for finite energy almost smooth solutions, ‖u‖$_{L^\infty_t L^2_x} +$ ‖$\nablau$‖$_{L^2_t L^2_x}$ ≲ $E(u_0,f,T)^{1/2}$. Automatically implies u $\in X^1$; also justifies the $L^2_t H^2_x$ condition in def:1.1 being redundant for $H^1$ solutions (cor:11.1 consequence).

thm:8.2.summary: Local energy estimate (new, main tool 1): for finite energy almost smooth solutions with divergence-free f, energy and dissipation in a shrunken ball $B(x_0,R$−r) are bounded by localised initial data, localised forcing, plus error terms $E^{1/2}T^{1/2}/r + E^{3/2}T^{1/2}/r^2$. Valid for large data and long times, unlike classical results. Prerequisite for enstrophy localisation.

prop:9.1.summary: Bounded total speed: ‖u‖$_{L^1_t L^\infty_x}$ ≲ $E^{1/2}T^{1/4} +$ E, where E $= E(u_0,f,T)$. Finite $L^1_t L^\infty_x$ control ensures fluid particles travel bounded distance, enabling material coordinates for tracking vorticity in the enstrophy localisation proof.

thm:10.1.summary: Enstrophy localisation (new, main tool 2): if initial vorticity is small on $B(x_0,R) ($‖$\omega_0$‖$_{L^2(B)} \le \delta$ with $\delta^4$ T $\ll 1$ and r > C(E $+ E^{1/2}T^{1/4} + \delta^{-2}))$, then ‖$\omega$‖$_{L^\infty_t L^2_x} +$ ‖$\nabla\omega$‖$_{L^2_t L^2_x}$ ≲ $\delta$ on $B(x_0,R$−$r) \times [0,T]$. Propagates vorticity smallness from t=0 across all times. The core technical engine of the paper.

prop:10.7.summary: Higher regularity from enstrophy localisation: under the same conditions as thm:10.1, all higher Sobolev norms ‖$\nabla^k$ u‖$_{L^\infty_t L^2_x(K)}$ are bounded on compact K $\subsetB(x_0,R$−r). Bootstraps the $L^2$ enstrophy bound to full local smoothness.

cor:11.1.summary: Bounded enstrophy: any finite energy almost smooth solution with $H^1$ initial data satisfies u $\in X^1([0,T]\timesR^3)$, making it a global $H^1$ solution. Proof: $H^1$ data means ‖$\omega_0$‖ $< \infty$, so $\omega_0$ is small outside a large ball; apply thm:10.1 on the complement to bound enstrophy globally.

cor:11.4.summary: Unconditional uniqueness: for smooth $H^1$ data, there is at most one almost smooth finite energy solution with normalised pressure. Proof: cor:11.1 upgrades almost smooth to mild $H^1$, then thm:5.4 uniqueness applies.

prop:11.6.summary: Uniform smoothness outside a ball: an incomplete almost smooth $H^1$ solution with blowup time T_* is smooth on $[0,T_*) \times$ K for all compact K $\subset R^3 \$ B(0,R). The singularity is spatially confined. Proof: outside a large ball, $H^1$ data makes initial vorticity small $\to$ enstrophy localisation $\to$ higher regularity.

prop:11.9.summary: Existence of partially smooth Leray-Hopf weak solutions: for smooth $H^1$ data, there exists a Leray-Hopf solution that is spatially smooth outside some ball B(0,R). Combines cor:11.1 (global $H^1$ existence) with prop:11.6 (smoothness outside ball).

lem:12.1.summary: Localisation of divergence-free vector fields: a smooth divergence-free field on an annulus satisfying zero normal flux (eq:104) can be truncated to vanish outside $R_3$ while remaining divergence-free and smooth, with $H^k$ norm loss of one derivative (eq:105). Converts spatially smooth $R^3$ data to compactly supported data comparable to periodic data.

thm:12.2.summary: Conditional: conjecture:1.9 implies conjecture:1.19. Under the assumption of global periodic $H^1$ well-posedness, any spatially smooth $H^1$ initial data on $R^3$ produces a global smooth solution. Proof uses lem:12.1 to localise $u_0$, then matches to a periodic problem via enstrophy localisation.

thm:13.1.summary: Conditional: conjecture:1.13 implies conjecture:1.14. Almost smooth global solutions from homogeneous $H^1$ data imply almost smooth global solutions from finite energy data. Uses enstrophy localisation to compare finite energy solutions with $H^1$ solutions on large balls.

prop:14.1.summary: Concentration compactness: conjecture:1.15 implies conjecture:1.16. Given a sequence with bounded $H^1$ norm, profile decomposition (prop:14.2) reduces global well-posedness to well-posedness for individual spatially separated profiles, each handled by thm:5.4.

prop:14.2.summary: Profile decomposition (Bahouri-Gérard type for $H^{100})$: bounded $H^{100}$ sequences decompose as $u_0^{(n)} = \Sigma \tau_{x_j^{(n)}} w_{j,0} + r^{(n)}_0$ with spatially separated profiles $(|x_j$ − $x_{j'}| \to \infty$, eq:121) and remainder with small $L^\infty$ norm (eq:120). Preserves divergence-free property.

prop:15.1.summary: Quantitative failure of regularity: for any smooth compactly supported $u_0, \psi$, R, M, $\varepsilon$, there exists perturbation $u_1$ with ‖$u_1$‖$_{H^1}$ ≲ $\varepsilon$ vanishing on B(0,R) such that the mild $H^1 (hence$ smooth by prop:5.6) solution from $u_0+u_1$ has $|\int\nabla\partial_t^2 p(0,x)\psi(x)dx| >$ M. Proves thm:1.12 by showing smooth $H^1$ data cannot guarantee smooth solutions.

edges.note: conj:$1.9 \to$ conj:1.19 proved in thm:12.2
conj:1.13 ↔ conj:1.14 equivalence established in thm:13.1
conj:1.13 ↔ conj:1.15 ↔ conj:1.16 ↔ conj:1.17 chain via concentration compactness in prop:14.1
conj:1.6 ↔ conj:1.4 equivalence via homogenisation in prop:1.7
conj:$1.19 \to$ conj:1.13 uses partially smooth Leray-Hopf solutions to connect spatially smooth and homogeneous $H^1$ data
conj:1.9 ↔ conj:1.10 uses unconditional uniqueness and the a priori bound structure
Non-existence of smooth solutions for some $H^1$ data follows from quantitative failure (prop:15.1)
Explicitly: mild $H^1$ solution with small data is smooth by prop:5.6; the constructed solution is smooth but has explosive pressure gradient at t=0
Irrational shift lemma provides the averaging sequence that shows forcing term averages to zero, enabling reduction to homogeneous data
Compactness of periodic $H^1$ solutions needed to pass to the limit in the homogenisation sequence
Localises spatially smooth $R^3$ data to compact support, making it comparable to periodic data on the torus
Enstrophy localisation ensures the periodic solution approximates the $R^3$ solution in the region of compact support
Partially smooth Leray-Hopf solution on $R^3$ is the comparison object: its smoothness outside a ball combined with the periodic solution gives global regularity
Periodic $H^1$ local well-posedness needed to construct the periodic comparison solutions used in the argument
Enstrophy localisation allows comparison between finite energy and homogeneous $H^1$ solutions on a large ball (vorticity small on complement)
Localises the finite energy initial data to match the homogeneous $H^1$ assumption on a bounded region
Profile decomposition reduces the compactness argument to individual spatially separated profiles
Local WP in $H^1$ applied to each individual translated profile $w_{j,0}$ independently
Heat semigroup estimates (energy-type) control the remainder $r^{(n)}_0$ and verify the $L^\infty_x$ smallness condition (eq:120)
Local energy estimate controls the energy flux through the ball boundary in the enstrophy Gronwall ODE, bounding the forcing term in the vorticity equation
Bounded total speed justifies material coordinate change: vorticity tracked along fluid trajectories, which stay within bounded displacement ≲ $E^{1/2}T^{1/4} +$ E (eq:79 condition)
Global energy bound $E(u_0,f,T)$ appears explicitly in the conditions (eq:78, eq:79) defining the safe enstrophy-localisation regime
Same smallness conditions as thm:10.1; bootstraps the $L^2$ enstrophy bound to $H^k$ bounds by Sobolev induction on the vorticity equation
Linear and bilinear estimates used in the higher-order vorticity bootstrap
$H^1$ data means ‖$\omega_0$‖$_{L^2} < \infty$, so $\omega_0$ is small outside a large ball; applies enstrophy localisation on complement to bound total enstrophy
Almost smooth solution must be almost smooth + mild (cor:4.3) to satisfy the hypotheses of thm:10.1 (which requires the mild formulation)
Bounded enstrophy (cor:11.1) places the almost smooth solution in $H^1$, enabling thm:5.4 uniqueness
Uniqueness for mild $H^1$ solutions invoked once cor:11.1 upgrades almost smooth to mild $H^1$
$H^1$ data decays at spatial $\infty \to$ initial vorticity is small on complement of a large ball $\to$ enstrophy localisation propagates smallness to all t $\in [0,T_*)$
Higher regularity proposition upgrades $L^2$ enstrophy bound outside B(0,R) to smooth control on compact subsets of the complement
Smoothness outside a ball from prop:11.6 provides the spatial smoothness component of the Leray-Hopf solution
Bounded enstrophy (cor:11.1) provides the global $H^1$ weak solution whose restriction outside B(0,R) is made smooth by prop:11.6
Global energy inequality controls the total energy E appearing in the error terms $(E^{1/2}T^{1/2}/r + E^{3/2}T^{1/2}/r^2)$ in the local estimate
Duhamel formula and linear estimates used in the localised energy Gronwall argument
$L^\infty_t L^2_x$ energy bound from lem:8.1 feeds into the interpolation giving $L^1_t L^\infty_x$ control via Bernstein/Sobolev
Exponential heat kernel decay controls high-frequency Littlewood-Paley blocks in the $L^\infty_x$ bound
Finite energy condition $E(u_0,f,T) < \infty (eq$:6 in def:1.1) is exactly what this lemma bounds
Bilinear estimate (eq:24) is the key nonlinear estimate in the contraction mapping for periodic $H^1$ LWP; eq:21 gives the linear part
Same bilinear contraction argument as thm:5.1, applied in $R^3$ instead of the torus
Maximal Cauchy development derived from the blowup alternative in thm:5.1 local existence
Local well-posedness used for each approximating sequence to produce solutions from weakly convergent data
Bilinear estimates needed to pass to the limit in the nonlinear term during weak compactness argument
Local $H^1$ WP gives short-time smooth existence; iterates on shrinking intervals to reach almost smooth on $(\tau,T]$
Calderon-Zygmund / Riesz transform estimates (covered by lem:2.1 framework) needed for the pressure formula in eq:40
Directly applies lem:4.1 to produce normalised pressure and hence the mild formulation from an almost smooth solution
Heat semigroup and $H^1$ estimates verify that shifted functions converge weakly in the appropriate function spaces

layer_1_problem_structure.description: NSE regularity landscape: solution categories, open conjectures, and the paper's two main results (implication diagram + non-existence theorem)

layer_2_analytic_tools.description: Foundational analysis: heat semigroup estimates, pressure normalisation, and mild formulation equivalence

layer_3_local_wellposedness.description: Local well-posedness in $H^1 (periodic$ and non-periodic) with compactness and almost-regularity consequences

layer_4_homogenisation.description: Homogenisation: converting forced periodic problems to unforced via irrational shifts

layer_5_energy_localisation.description: New localised energy estimates valid for large data / long times — the paper's first major new tool

layer_6_enstrophy_localisation.description: Enstrophy localisation theorem — propagation of vorticity smallness from initial data across all times in a ball — the paper's central new tool

layer_7_global_consequences.description: Global consequences of enstrophy localisation: bounded enstrophy, unconditional uniqueness, singularity confinement, partially smooth weak solutions

layer_8_implication_proofs.description: Conditional implication proofs (conj:$1.9 \to$ conj:1.19; conj:1.13 ↔ conj:1.14; concentration compactness equivalences) and quantitative non-existence construction

feff.title: Existence and smoothness of the Navier-Stokes equation (Clay Millennium Prize problem statement)

tao-quantitative.title: A quantitative formulation of the global regularity problem for the periodic Navier-Stokes equation

leray.title: Sur le mouvement d'un liquide visqueux emplissant l'espace (Leray 1934 — original NSE weak solutions)

ckn.title: Partial regularity of suitable weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations (Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg)

ess.title: $L^{3,\infty}$-solutions of Navier-Stokes equations and backward uniqueness (Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak)

rusin.title: Minimal initial data for potential Navier-Stokes singularities (Rusin-Sverák)

gallagher.title: Profile decomposition for solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations (Gallagher 2001)

bahouri.title: High frequency approximation of solutions to critical nonlinear wave equations (Bahouri-Gérard 1999)

bkm.title: Remarks on the breakdown of smooth solutions for the 3-D Euler equations (Beale-Kato-Majda)

serrin.title: On the interior regularity of weak solutions of the Navier-Stokes equations (Serrin 1962)
